column2: to protect our citizens online
column3: as we do offline. Second: to defend our autonomy in setting our own rules. The European Union is today a global leader in digital regulation. We have adopted groundbreaking legislation such as the Digital Services Act and the Digital Markets Act. Yet
column4: with leadership comes responsibility not only to regulate
column5: but also to ensure that regulation has a direct impact on people's lives.

column1: The Digital Services Act aims to create a safe online environment for our citizens. Platforms have to remove illegal content and address the risk of disinformation and other harmful material. At the same time
column2: the Digital Markets Act ensures that large platforms – the so-called gatekeepers – behave in a fair way online. Both of these acts set a high global benchmark. They establish clear obligations based on the importance of the online platform. In other words
column3: bigger players have bigger responsibilities.

column1: But these laws are now being contested. The US President has threatened tariffs against the EU. Large technology firms also resist. A recent example is Apple's call to scrap the Digital Markets Act. What I can say to these statements is that the rules apply regardless of the headquarters of the companies. They apply equally to companies of European
column2: Chinese or US origin. To put it in short
column3: the rules do not discriminate. They try to create a level playing field. Doing business in the EU is highly profitable for digital players. The single market is attractive for platforms
column4: in particular for many US large online platforms and search engines. The cost of compliance with the DSA and the DMA is very low compared to the financial benefits of operating in the single market.

column1: The EU has the sovereign right to regulate economic activities within its territory
column2: in line with our own democratic values. We have always enforced and will continue to enforce our laws in a fair manner towards all companies operating in the EU. This is done in full compliance with global rules and in regular contact with online platforms. As in all other policy areas
column3: we will continue to take the decisions we think are needed to ensure a fair and safe digital space. We count on the European Parliament as a counterpart in this effort.""";
